extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine with variables opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks (int [0,10]), initialized to false/false/0. The property requires globally (opted_out implies not sent): no reachable state may have opted_out=true and sent=true.

The transition t_resume_glitch [on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 10): sent := true] directly sets sent:=true while opted_out is true, contradicting the property. The remaining question is reachability and whether this firing is permissible. On send_attempt with opted_out true and ticks>=10, both t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) and t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out and ticks>=10) are enabled; the spec gives no strict priority, so t_resume_glitch firing is a valid execution, and the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution.

Witness execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
- Initial: opted_out=F, sent=F, ticks=0
- tick x10 (t_tick): ticks increments each time, capping at 10; sent set to F each time. Result: opted_out=F, sent=F, ticks=10
- optout (t_optout): opted_out:=T, sent:=F. Result: opted_out=T, sent=F, ticks=10
- send_attempt: t_resume_glitch guard (opted_out=T AND ticks=10>=10) holds, so sent:=T. Result: opted_out=T, sent=T, ticks=10

The final state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating opted_out implies not sent. Property VIOLATED.
